title: Write Tomorrow's Three Priorities, Close the Notebook
subtitle: A practical wind-down activity for offloading the mental list before sleep
excerpt: A ten-minute activity for Day 7 of the sleep arc. Write three priorities for tomorrow, then close the notebook. The brain stops tracking when it knows the list is written somewhere it can trust.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Built from the Day 7 theme of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798269649795). The three-priority technique draws on productivity research (Ivy Lee method, 1918; various adaptations in modern sleep-hygiene literature).

practice.whenToUse: Any evening — especially one where you arrive at bed still mentally running the list. Do this before the bedroom, not in it.

content.text: A simple activity for Day 7. You need a notebook and a pen. The brain keeps running the list because it doesn't trust that anything is being tracked. Writing three clear priorities gives it somewhere to park — so it can stop.
Ten minutes in the evening, away from the bedroom. Write tomorrow's three most important tasks — the ones that, if done, would make the day a success. Three only. Not ten, not five. Three.

The mind keeps looping because it's trying to hold everything — the incomplete items, the half-started tasks, the things that got moved again. Writing the three priorities signals to the brain that the tracking work is done. It doesn't need to keep running the list all night. A physical notebook (not a phone app) works better: the act of writing by hand does something the phone can't quite replicate.
Sit somewhere that isn't bed. Notebook in front of you.
Write tomorrow's date at the top of a fresh page.
Write three things — numbered, specific. "Email Sarah re Thursday" is better than "emails".

If more than three things come up, ask: if I could only do one tomorrow, which one? Then which one after that? Then the third. The rest goes on a separate running list if needed — not in tonight's priorities.
Close the notebook. Don't reopen it tonight.

The urge to add a fourth item is strong. Resist it — the practice only works with the constraint. Some people find the list takes thirty seconds once they get used to it; others need the full ten minutes to decide. Both are fine.

(Rebecca J Page, 2025). The three-priority structure draws on the Ivy Lee method (1918) and its adaptations in modern productivity and sleep-hygiene literature.
